zotero.abstractNote: We consider the problem of coordinating users of shared facilities, such as scientific instruments or meeting rooms. Users have individual preferences and constraints, and they compete for time slots. We propose a system where a predictive assistant learns user preferences from historical data and suggests schedules that are socially optimal. We model the interaction between the assistant and the users as a game, and we show that our approach leads to a stable and efficient allocation of resources. We evaluate our method on real-world data from a shared facility and demonstrate that it significantly improves user satisfaction compared to existing scheduling policies.
